D3 Chemistry L4 Analyze Reference

Pigment Incompatibility — Lead + Sulfur Goes Black

historic combinations spell trouble: white lead next to ultramarine darkens at the boundary because PbS forms; cadmium yellow + copper green also degrade slowly

Some pigment pairs are chemically incompatible. Lead carbonate (white lead) reacts with atmospheric H₂S or sulphide pigments (vermilion, ultramarine) to form black lead sulphide. Copper-based greens darken with sulphur. Cadmium yellow next to copper green slowly forms cadmium-copper double salts. Old-master conservators watch these interfaces.

Pick a famous pairing. The simulated 50-year boundary darkening shows the affected interface as a black band; the diagnostic text identifies the reaction product.
